MOTION / Notice Of Motion & Motion To Continue Hearing And Amend Complaint Under Ccp 437c(H)
Off Calendar Mar-02-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Atty. JAMES L. ARMSTRONG, Esq. of Law Offices of Andrew Wolff, PC (Tel: 510-834-3300) for plaintiff Eli Hall;
Atty. WILLIAM CORUM, Esq. of Husch Blackwell LLP (Tel: 816-983-8000) for defendant See's Candies.

Hearing Re: Plaintiff Eli Hall's Motion To Continue Hearing And Amend Complaint Under CCP 437c(h).

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff's "motion to continue hearing and amend complaint" under Code of Civil Procedure section 437c(h)" is denied. The motion is brought on the ground that "Plaintiff needs to add to the record the facts asserted in his (Proposed) Amended Complaint in order to have a chance to defeat defendant's MSJ." However, section 437c(h) does not authorize the relief Plaintiff seeks. It provides that if a party opposing a motion for summary judgment shows that "facts essential to justify opposition may exist but cannot, for reasons stated, be presented, the court shall deny the motion, order a continuance to permit affidavits to be obtained or discovery to be had, or make any other order as may be just." In other words, "To be entitled to a continuance, the party opposing the motion for summary judgment must show that its proposed discovery would have led to facts essential to justify opposition." (Scott v. CIBA Vision Corp. (1995) 38 Cal.App.4th 307, 325-326.) Plaintiff is not seeking a continuance to obtain additional discovery (nor could he, because the co-employee in question is deceased). Rather, he contends only that his counsel "realized, very recently," that he could have pled a different theory and different cause of action (for Defendant's purported ratification of an intentional assault on Plaintiff by his co-employee) than the negligence and premises liability causes of action Plaintiff pled in the complaint, which was filed on March 28, 2019. (Armstrong Decl.) However, Section 437c(h) does not allow a party to avoid summary judgment by belatedly concocting a new cause of action that is outside the scope of the pleading on which summary judgment is sought. Further, even if Plaintiff had made the showing required by the statute, "lack of diligence may be a ground for denying a request for a continuance." (Rodriguez v. Oto (2013) 212 Cal.App.4th 1020, 1038.) The record is clear that the facts have been known to Plaintiff since the incident occurred on November 17, 2017. Plaintiff did not exercise diligence.

To the extent that Plaintiff is seeking leave to file an amended complaint, his motion is untimely, as it was not filed and served at least 16 court days before the date of the hearing (Code Civ. Proc. sec. 1005(b)), and it fails to comply with the Rules of Court (Cal. R. Ct. 3.1324). Further, "even if a good amendment is proposed in proper form, unwarranted delay in presenting it may-of itself-be a valid reason for denial." (Record v. Reason (1999) 73 Cal.App.4th 472, 486.) Here, Plaintiff's proposed amendment is not based on any new facts that he was not aware of in 2017, and his counsel's failure to "discover" a novel theory earlier does not justify his delay. "Further, the facts as developed in the summary judgment motion do not show [intentional assault or ratification]. Leave to amend is properly denied where the facts are undisputed and as a substantive matter no liability exists under the plaintiff's new theory." (Huff v. Wilkins (2006) 138 Cal.App.4th 743, 765.)

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR No. 8600, Email: maria.torreano@gmail.com (302/EPS)

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
Off Calendar Mar-02-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Atty. JAMES L. ARMSTRONG, Esq. of Law Offices of Andrew Wolff, PC (Tel: 510-834-3300) for plaintiff Eli Hall;
Atty. WILLIAM CORUM, Esq. of Husch Blackwell LLP (Tel: 816-983-8000) for defendant See's Candies.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT SEE'S CANDIES, INC.'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant See's Candies, Inc.'s motion for summary judgment is granted, on two independent grounds. First, it is undisputed that Plaintiff is a special employee of Defendant. Accordingly, his exclusive remedy is through workers' compensation. (Lab. Code sec. 3600(d)(1); Ybarra v. John Bean Techs. Corp. (E.D.Cal. 2012) 853 F.Supp.2d 997, 1014; Angelotti v. The Walt Disney Co. (2011) 192 Cal.App.4th 1394, 1403-1406.) Second, Plaintiff acknowledged in his employment agreement that workers' compensation was his sole remedy for workplace injuries, and expressly waived any claims against Defendant. (Employment Agreement par. 17.) Plaintiff does not dispute any of the material facts on which the motion is brought, nor does he oppose the motion on these grounds. Accordingly, it must be granted.

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR No. 8600, Email: maria.torreano@gmail.com (302/EPS)
